venues.name: Daytona International Speedway
venues.description: The most iconic oval in American motorsport. Daytona International Speedway has hosted NASCAR's premier event — the Daytona 500 — since 1959. The 2.5-mile high-banked superspeedway produces pack racing at close to 200mph where drafting, positioning, and survival matter more than outright speed. One wrong move at 195mph in a 40-car pack ends your day. The banking in the turns reaches 31 degrees, allowing cars to maintain full throttle nearly everywhere on the circuit.
venues.circuit_notes: Restrictor plate racing means the entire field runs at near-identical speeds. Drafting is everything — finding the right push partner on the final lap often decides the race. The trioval start-finish line, the famous frontstretch, and the backstretch banking are all at slightly different angles, creating an irregular feel despite the oval shape.
legendary_moments: Richard Petty's record 7th Daytona 500 win in 1979
Dale Earnhardt Sr.'s emotional 1998 Daytona 500 win after 20 attempts
Denny Hamlin's photo-finish wins in 2016 and 2019
Tyler Reddick's dominant 2026 Daytona 500 victory

venues.name: Atlanta Motor Speedway
venues.description: Rebuilt and reconfigured in 2022 into the second-fastest track on the NASCAR calendar. The repave and banking changes turned what was a conventional 1.54-mile intermediate into a superspeedway-style experience with drafting packs running at 190mph. The narrow groove and steep banking create intense, chaotic pack racing reminiscent of Daytona and Talladega — except on a significantly shorter circuit. Lap times are among the lowest in NASCAR, and the action is relentless.
venues.circuit_notes: The 2022 repave dramatically changed the character of Atlanta. Cars now run in a tight pack with side-by-side racing through all four turns. Tire fall-off is significant, making pit strategy crucial. The track is abrasive enough that tire management separates the field late in each stage.
legendary_moments: Jeff Gordon's first-ever Cup Series win, Atlanta 1994
Bobby Labonte's dominant 1999-2000 Atlanta performances
The chaotic 2022 repave race that redefined the track
Tyler Reddick's back-to-back win in 2026

venues.name: Circuit of the Americas
venues.description: The longest circuit on the NASCAR Cup Series calendar and the only Formula 1-specification road course on the schedule. COTA's 20-turn layout winds through the Texas Hill Country west of Austin, featuring the dramatic uphill run to Turn 1, the Esses complex, and the fast back section. NASCAR stock cars handle completely differently to the single-seaters COTA was designed for — their size, weight, and tyre compounds make every corner an exercise in commitment and car control.
venues.circuit_notes: Turn 1 is the great leveller — the braking zone from 170mph into the sharp left is where positions are won and lost on lap one. The Esses (Turns 3-10) demand smooth inputs; jerky corrections in a stock car here are race-ending. The hairpin at Turn 12 is a prime overtaking spot with a long approach and late braking. Road course specialists and ringers from other categories regularly appear.
legendary_moments: Chase Elliott's dominant debut Cup win at COTA in 2021
Ross Chastain's wall-riding Hail Mary at COTA 2022
Denny Hamlin's 2026 victory over Chase Elliott

venues.name: Phoenix Raceway
venues.description: The Championship Decider. Phoenix Raceway hosts the penultimate race of the NASCAR Championship, meaning the track has determined multiple Cup Series titles across the modern era. The flat, D-shaped one-mile oval is deceptive in its demands — the low banking of just 11 degrees means mechanical grip and setup precision matter far more than aerodynamic advantages. Passing is difficult, making track position and pit strategy the decisive factors.
venues.circuit_notes: Phoenix's unique shape means Turn 1 and Turn 2 are flat and sweeping, while Turns 3 and 4 are tighter. The dogleg coming off Turn 2 catches drivers out at full speed. The track surface tends to get slick as the race matures, rewarding patience and tire management. Starts and restarts are crucial — gaining or losing spots in the first few laps of each stage can be season-defining.
legendary_moments: Jeff Gordon clinching championships at Phoenix multiple times
Kevin Harvick's decade of Phoenix dominance
Kyle Larson's 2021 championship clinch
Ryan Blaney's 2026 Straight Talk Wireless 500 win over Christopher Bell

venues.name: Las Vegas Motor Speedway
venues.description: The quintessential 1.5-mile intermediate oval. Las Vegas Motor Speedway sits in the Nevada desert northeast of the Strip and hosts two Cup Series races per year. Smooth, wide and technically demanding, Vegas rewards the best-prepared cars more than anywhere else. The 20-degree banking allows multiple lanes of racing through the corners, and the long straightaways produce genuine top-speed differentials between teams.
venues.circuit_notes: Tyre degradation is moderate at Vegas, meaning four-tyre pit stops typically win over two-tyre calls. The critical decision is usually which stage to prioritise. Drivers who can maintain consistent lap times without overworking the right-front tyre tend to dominate. The pit road entrance and exit on the backstretch can be treacherous, and several races have been decided by late pit penalties.
legendary_moments: Denny Hamlin's multiple Las Vegas victories for Joe Gibbs Racing
Josh Berry's 2025 win for Wood Brothers Racing
Hamlin's 61st career win at Vegas in 2026, surpassing Kevin Harvick on the all-time list

venues.name: Darlington Raceway
venues.description: Too Tough to Tame. The Lady in Black. Darlington Raceway is unlike any other oval in NASCAR — asymmetrical, abrasive, and absolutely unforgiving. The 1.366-mile egg-shaped track is a genuine challenge even for the best drivers in the world. The concrete wall sits just inches from the racing line in Turns 3 and 4, and virtually every car leaves Darlington with at least one scuff or stripe — the infamous 'Darlington Stripe' earned by grazing the outside wall.
venues.circuit_notes: The two ends of Darlington are completely different. Turns 1 and 2 have a tighter radius and more banking than Turns 3 and 4, which are shallower and wider. This asymmetry forces drivers to use different cornering styles at each end. Tyre wear is brutal — right-rear tyres can be consumed in as few as 30 laps. Fuel strategy is less important than tyre strategy here.
legendary_moments: Richard Petty's record 10 wins at Darlington
The Southern 500 tradition on Labor Day weekend
Brad Keselowski leading 142 laps but finishing 2nd in 2026
Tyler Reddick overcoming a battery change to win the 2026 Goodyear 400

records.most_wins: David Pearson — 10 wins, tied with Richard Petty

venues.name: Martinsville Speedway
venues.description: The oldest active track in NASCAR and the most intimate racing experience on the calendar. Martinsville's paperclip shape is just 0.526 miles around — 40 cars packed onto a tiny Virginia bullring where bump-drafting, door-banging, and retribution are all fair game. The track was built in 1947 by local businessman H. Clay Earles and has hosted NASCAR racing every year since 1949. Winners receive a handmade grandfather clock from a local manufacturer — a tradition dating back to 1964.
venues.circuit_notes: Braking from 120mph into Martinsville's flat corners is savage. The concrete in the corners and asphalt on the straights create a mixed-surface challenge that wears tyres differently. Track position is everything — with limited overtaking opportunity, the team that executes on pit road usually wins. The last 50 laps are almost always a contact sport.
legendary_moments: Rusty Wallace's four consecutive spring Martinsville wins 1993-1996
Jeff Gordon and Jimmie Johnson's epic 2007 title battle
Matt Kenseth wrecking Joey Logano for revenge in 2015
Denny Hamlin's record-tying six Martinsville wins
The grandfather clock tradition since 1964

venues.name: Bristol Motor Speedway
venues.description: The Last Great Colosseum. Bristol Motor Speedway is the most electric atmosphere in NASCAR — 162,000 fans packed into the Tennessee mountains for what amounts to gladiatorial combat on concrete. The 28-degree banking is steep enough that cars can run flat-out everywhere on the circuit, producing speeds that feel outrageous for a half-mile oval. The night race under Bristol's lights is a genuine bucket-list event for any motorsport fan.
venues.circuit_notes: The concrete surface is extremely aggressive on tyres, particularly the right-front. Overdriving the entry in the tight corners kills tyres fast. The track is just wide enough for two lanes, meaning racing three-wide is genuinely dangerous but happens regularly. Lapped traffic is a massive factor — the leaders spend most of the race in heavy traffic, and the management of that traffic separates winners from contenders.
legendary_moments: The Earnhardt-Gordon war of the 1990s
Darrell Waltrip's record 12 wins at Bristol
Matt Kenseth dominating the 2012 night race from the rear
The Bristol dirt race era 2021-2024
Kyle Larson's multiple Bristol night race victories

venues.name: Talladega Superspeedway
venues.description: The biggest and most dangerous oval in NASCAR. At 2.66 miles with 33-degree banking, Talladega Superspeedway is the ultimate test of nerve, positioning, and survival. The Big One — a massive multi-car pileup that typically takes out a third of the field — is a near-certainty at some point in every race. Nobody knows where it will happen, or to whom. This unpredictability is exactly what makes Talladega the greatest show in American motorsport.
venues.circuit_notes: With restrictor plates equalising the field, Talladega is the great leveller. A last-place team can win if they draft well and survive. The key skill is positioning — staying near the front of the pack without being at the absolute front where the air is thin. The draft line and slingshot moves in the final laps are chess at 195mph.
legendary_moments: Bobby Allison's final race ending in tragedy, 1988
Dale Earnhardt Sr.'s mastery of the draft
Michael Waltrip's emotional 2001 Daytona 500 win (same technique applied)
Brad Keselowski's 2009 breakthrough win
The Great American Wreck compilation — every Talladega Big One ever

venues.name: Kansas Speedway
venues.description: One of the newer 1.5-mile ovals on the NASCAR calendar, Kansas Speedway opened in 2001 and has quickly become a playoff staple. The wide, smooth racing surface allows for genuine multi-groove racing, and the 17-degree banking is shallow enough that mechanical grip plays a bigger role than at steeper tracks. Two Cup Series races per year — one regular season, one playoff — means Kansas has seen some of the most important moments of recent championships.
venues.circuit_notes: Kansas produces some of the most variable racing of any intermediate circuit. The track can create a dominant lane early in a run, then shift as fuel loads change and tyres wear. Teams that can adapt their set-up during a long green-flag run often emerge with superior positions. The turn entry on Turns 1 and 2 is particularly sensitive to setup balance.
legendary_moments: Denny Hamlin's multiple Kansas victories
Joey Logano's 2022 playoff clincher
Multiple overtime finishes in playoff races

venues.name: Charlotte Motor Speedway
venues.description: Home base. Charlotte Motor Speedway sits in the heart of NASCAR country and hosts the longest race on the calendar — the Coca-Cola 600 on Memorial Day weekend at 600 miles. With most teams headquartered within 30 miles of the track, this is the race every driver and crew chief wants to win. Charlotte also hosts the All-Star Race and has run a road course variant using the infield in recent seasons.
venues.circuit_notes: The Coca-Cola 600 is a race of survival as much as speed — 600 miles under Memorial Day heat tests both machinery and personnel. Night racing adds a layer of complexity as temperatures drop and grip levels change. The track is technically demanding at the entry of turns, where oversteer can be career-ending at 170mph.
legendary_moments: Jeff Gordon's four Coca-Cola 600 wins
Kasey Kahne's 2008 All-Star Race win
Jimmie Johnson's Charlotte mastery in his championship years
Denny Hamlin's 600-mile survival wins

venues.name: Watkins Glen International
venues.description: Historic road racing in the Finger Lakes. Watkins Glen International has been hosting racing since 1948 and is one of the most respected circuits in North American motorsport. The bus stop chicane, the uphill esses, and the sweeping final corner combine to create a genuinely technical challenge. Stock cars at Watkins Glen are a spectacular sight — 3,400-pound machines with all the finesse of a freight train navigating corners designed for purpose-built race cars.
venues.circuit_notes: The bus stop chicane is optional for NASCAR — most drivers run straight through the slow kerbed section and take the penalty. The decision to use it or not depends on tyre compound and race position. The esses require smooth, committed driving. The final corner is a long, sweeping 180-degree right-hander that rewards both set-up and bravery.
legendary_moments: Mark Martin's five victories at Watkins Glen 1993-1997
Jeff Gordon winning from the back
AJ Allmendinger's upset 2014 win
Chase Elliott's road course mastery at the Glen

venues.name: Sonoma Raceway
venues.description: Wine country road racing. Sonoma Raceway's rolling hills, elevation changes, and technical layout make it one of the most demanding road courses on the NASCAR calendar. The carousel complex is the most photographed section of any road course in American motorsport — a sweeping series of left-handers that demands both mechanical grip and driver commitment. Racing at Sonoma always produces drama: wheel-to-wheel through the hairpin, aggressive braking into Turn 11, and inevitably, at least one spectacular off.
venues.circuit_notes: Sonoma uses several different configurations for different events. The NASCAR layout typically runs the longer variation including the chute and the backstretch esses. Fuel conservation often plays a role — teams have stretched fuel windows at Sonoma to gain track position. The brake zones are severe, particularly at Turn 7 where cars shed massive speed in a short distance.
legendary_moments: Jeff Gordon's five Sonoma wins — his favourite road course
Tony Stewart's back-to-back wins 2011-2012
Kyle Larson's dominant 2021 performance

venues.name: Indianapolis Motor Speedway
venues.description: The Racing Capital of the World. Indianapolis Motor Speedway is the most famous motorsport venue on the planet. NASCAR races both the oval and the road course at Indy — the oval with its near-flat 9-degree banking creates a very different challenge to the steep superspeedways. The yard of bricks at the start-finish line, a tradition dating back to the early 1900s, demands that winners kneel and kiss the surface. Standing at the Speedway for the first time is a religious experience.
venues.circuit_notes: On the oval, the shallow 9-degree banking means teams cannot run wide-open through the corners — set-up precision and downforce balance are critical. The road course weaves through the infield and incorporates the famous main straight, creating a genuinely fast and technical combined layout.
legendary_moments: Jeff Gordon's Brickyard 400 dynasty — four wins
Dale Earnhardt's emotional first Brickyard win in 1995
The controversial 2008 race stopped for tyre failures
Kyle Larson's 2021 road course win — his best of the season

venues.name: Richmond Raceway
venues.description: Saturday night short-track action in Virginia. Richmond Raceway's three-quarter-mile D-shaped oval sits just outside the state capital and has hosted NASCAR racing since 1953. The Saturday night race tradition stretches back decades and gives Richmond a unique, old-school atmosphere. Two Cup Series dates per year — one spring, one regular-season finale — mean Richmond has served as the final regular-season cutoff point, producing enormous late-race drama.
venues.circuit_notes: Richmond is wide enough for two grooves but rarely three. The outside lane is often faster early in a run, while the bottom becomes dominant as tyres wear and the track rubberers up. Caution timing is crucial — teams that can time their pit stops to avoid the wave of pit road traffic gain positions without passing anyone on track.
legendary_moments: Dale Earnhardt's battles with the field in his Richmond prime
Denny Hamlin's multiple Richmond victories for JGR
Multiple drivers making the regular-season cut by dramatic margins

venues.name: Dover Motor Speedway
venues.description: The Monster Mile. Dover Motor Speedway's concrete one-mile oval with 24-degree banking is one of the most physically demanding tracks in NASCAR. The concrete surface generates massive tyre wear and produces high grip early in a run that fades dramatically as tyres overheat. Speeds approach 160mph through the corners, which feels extraordinary on a one-mile oval. The Monster Energy drink mascot — the 'Miles the Monster' figure — watches over the start-finish line.
venues.circuit_notes: Dover rewards committed drivers who can preserve their right-front tyre while still pushing hard. The concrete banking allows full commitment through the corners but punishes any lapse in concentration — the barriers are close and the wall is unyielding concrete. Passing is possible on both lanes, but patience and choosing the right moment to attack are essential.
legendary_moments: Jimmie Johnson's record 11 wins at Dover — the Monster Mile King
Jeff Gordon's multiple dominant performances
Multiple overtime finishes in the modern era

venues.name: Michigan International Speedway
venues.description: Two miles of smooth Michigan asphalt in the Irish Hills. Michigan International Speedway is the widest track on the NASCAR circuit, stretching to 72 feet in places, which allows genuine multi-lane racing through all four corners. Top speeds approach 215mph on a good day, and the smooth, sweeping turns reward long throttle applications that few other tracks allow. The result is one of the purest tests of raw outright speed in American motorsport.
venues.circuit_notes: The wide surface at Michigan creates three or even four lanes of viable racing. Setup balance through the long, sweeping turns is critical — understeer at high speed becomes dangerous quickly. Teams often run more downforce than expected because the corner commitment required in the wide, fast turns benefits from mechanical grip.
legendary_moments: David Pearson vs. Richard Petty's legendary 1976 finish
Greg Biffle's Michigan dominance in the mid-2000s
Kyle Larson's multiple 200mph+ qualifying laps

venues.name: Pocono Raceway
venues.description: The Tricky Triangle. Pocono Raceway's unique triangular 2.5-mile layout has three completely different corners — the Tunnel Turn, the Carousel, and the North Turn — that require a completely different setup compromise to any other track. No corner is like the one before it, and no set-up decision comes without a trade-off somewhere else on the lap. Pocono is genuinely unique in world motorsport.
venues.circuit_notes: Pocono's three corners have different banking angles, radii, and surface characteristics. The Tunnel Turn is the fastest and most committed, taken at speeds approaching 200mph. The Carousel is tighter and slower, generating massive tyre loads. The North Turn is a sweeping, long right-hander that challenges mechanical grip and driver bravery equally. Getting all three right in one setup is almost impossible.
legendary_moments: Ryan Newman's multiple Pocono victories
Jeff Gordon vs. Jimmie Johnson Pocono battles
Kyle Busch's dominance of the Poconos in the late 2010s

venues.name: New Hampshire Motor Speedway
venues.description: The Magic Mile. New Hampshire Motor Speedway's flat, low-banked one-mile oval in the New England hills is one of the most technically demanding tracks in NASCAR. Just 12 degrees of banking means aerodynamic downforce is nearly irrelevant — it's all about mechanical grip, set-up precision, and driver skill through the flat, slippery corners. This is a track where the best drivers and the best-prepared cars consistently emerge, regardless of the horsepower package.
venues.circuit_notes: The flat banking at New Hampshire means the cars push significantly through the corners, loading the right-front tyre heavily. Teams that can manage right-front tyre wear while still maintaining competitive lap times gain a decisive advantage in the final stage. Restarts are chaotic because the flat corners reward inside-line defenders.
legendary_moments: Jeff Burton's New Hampshire dominance in the late 1990s
Jimmie Johnson's multiple Magic Mile wins
Kyle Busch's consistent strong showings

race_2026.name: NASCAR Cup Race at New Hampshire

venues.name: Homestead-Miami Speedway
venues.description: The Championship Finale. Homestead-Miami Speedway returns to the role it played from 2002 to 2019 as the final race of the NASCAR season and the venue where the Cup Series champion is crowned. The 1.5-mile oval in South Florida combines high banking with a smooth, progressive racing surface that rewards commitment. It has produced some of the most dramatic championship finales in NASCAR history, with four title contenders often separated by fractions going into the final laps.
venues.circuit_notes: Homestead's progressive banking — varying from 18 to 20 degrees through the turns — creates a wide, grippy surface in the high lane. Teams often run more downforce than at similar-length tracks because the banking rewards it. Tyre wear is moderate, and two-stop strategies are viable. The final race atmosphere is electric — whoever crosses the finish line first among the championship contenders wins it all.
legendary_moments: Tony Stewart's dramatic three-way championship duel, 2011
Kevin Harvick's dominance at Homestead
Jimmie Johnson's multiple championship clinches
Kyle Busch's emotional 2015 championship comeback win
The 2026 Championship Race — to be decided
